title: SBC President Bart Barber Gives a Lesson in How Not to Repent
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman dissects SBC President Bart Barber's October 2022 'repentance' for his Twitter attacks on Pastor Dusty Deevers, arguing that partial confession, performative grief, and continued finger-pointing constitute non-repentance and undermine the kind of biblical contrition the SBC desperately needs. Using Barber's thread as a case study, the article articulates an abolitionist theology of repentance and links it to the prospects for revival in the convention.
audiences: SBC pastor or member weighing how to respond to denominational leadership
Abolitionist activist confronting pro-life establishment opposition
Pastor or counselor teaching biblical repentance
Pro-life Christian unfamiliar with the Louisiana bill controversy
Skeptic of abolitionism investigating claims about coerced minors and ectopic pregnancies

questions.question: What specific event triggered Bart Barber's public 'repentance,' and what only did he actually retract?
questions.audience: SBC pastor or member weighing how to respond to denominational leadership
questions.answer: On September 15, 2022, Barber launched a Twitter attack on SBC pastor Dusty Deevers (followed the next day by a thread against the Abolitionist Movement broadly). Weeks later he posted a Twitter 'repentance' that retracted only one word, 'obsession,' while leaving every other slanderous claim intact.
questions.quote: On September 15, Southern Baptist Convention (SBC) President Bart Barber [unleashed](https://twitter.com/bartbarber/status/1570377775030349827) a vicious, dishonest Twitter attack against SBC pastor Dusty Deevers.

questions.question: Why does Silberman call partial repentance no repentance at all?
questions.audience: Pastor or counselor teaching biblical repentance
questions.answer: Drawing on Joel Lindsey at The Gospel Coalition, Silberman argues that genuine repentance requires full confession of every sin committed; throwing a bone to critics while refusing to name the actual offenses is not repentance but a public-relations gesture.
questions.quote: Deep repentance demands full confession. Though it seems counterintuitive, the only way to be truly covered by Christ is to fully expose your sin. In the process of repentance, we must fight to be utterly transparent before God about the depth and breadth of our sin. Only ruthless honesty will suffice—and lead to freedom and joy.

questions.question: What is the most serious unrepented slander the article identifies in Barber's thread?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activist confronting pro-life establishment opposition
questions.answer: Silberman points to Barber's accusation that Deevers wants to imprison coerced 16-year-old girls who have abortions. He calls this the most viciously slanderous sentence of both threads and notes Deevers has never said anything that could even remotely suggest such a position.
questions.quote: [U]nless you 100% agree with every jot and tittle of Deevers's obsession with sending 16-year-old girls to prison for succumbing to the coercion of their parents to have an abortion, he will label you 'against the innocent preborn.'

questions.question: How does the article say Barber misrepresented Deevers's original criticism of Brent Leatherwood?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian unfamiliar with the Louisiana bill controversy
questions.answer: Deevers's tweet specifically charged Leatherwood with siding against the preborn in Louisiana and at the SBC annual meeting. Barber stripped that quote from its Louisiana context and framed it as if Deevers were accusing the entire SBC of opposing the preborn.
questions.quote: Barber extracted Deevers' quotation from its context and applied it as if Deevers was leveling the accusation against the entire convention.

questions.question: What role did the Ethics and Religious Liberty Commission play in the Louisiana abolition bill?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian unfamiliar with the Louisiana bill controversy
questions.answer: Brent Leatherwood, before being hired as ERLC president, signed an open letter during Louisiana's deliberation pledging that the ERLC would never support any law that allowed prosecution of women who have abortions. The bill had sailed through committee with universal Republican support, and Silberman argues it likely would have passed had the ERLC and other pro-life groups not publicly opposed it.
questions.quote: Given the bill's easy passage through committee, it's safe to say there's a good chance it would have passed had the ERLC and other pro-life groups not sabotaged it.

questions.question: How does the article rebut Barber's claim that abolition bills ignore ectopic pregnancies?
questions.audience: Skeptic of abolitionism investigating claims about coerced minors and ectopic pregnancies
questions.answer: Silberman points to screenshots from Bradley Pierce of Abolish Abortion Texas showing actual bill language, and notes Pierce (the drafter of most abolition bills) also offered Barber a phone call to discuss them, which Barber opened but never answered.
questions.quote: Pierce, an attorney and the drafter of most abolition bills, also messaged Barber to ask him for a phone call to discuss the screenshots, messages which Barber opened but did not respond to.

questions.question: Why does Silberman call Barber's attack on abolitionists' motives an aggravation rather than an isolated mistake?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activist confronting pro-life establishment opposition
questions.answer: Barber didn't merely misstate the content of abolition bills; he leveraged that misrepresentation to accuse abolitionists of deliberate deception, telling readers that abolitionists' tweets and laws say different things on purpose.
questions.quote: If an abortion abolitionist succeeds at the legislature, your state won't be governed by his tweets; you'll be governed by his laws. He knows that full well. You deserve to know it, too.

questions.question: How does the article distinguish godly grief from worldly grief in repentance?
questions.audience: Pastor or counselor teaching biblical repentance
questions.answer: Quoting 2 Corinthians 7, Silberman defines true repentance as spirit-wrought, mournful over harm done to the victim, eager to confess all guilt, indignant at sin, and zealous to restore. Performative repentance, by contrast, is staged for an audience and aimed at managing reputation rather than restoring the wronged party.
questions.quote: True repentance is spirit-wrought and heart-felt. It mourns the harm committed against the victim. It is eager to confess all guilt. It is indignant at the sin that made it necessary. It is zealous to restore what was broken. It fears the holiness of a watching God. Performative repentance is a public relations show put on for the watching world.

questions.question: What practical evidence does Silberman cite that Barber's repentance was directed at his audience rather than the victim?
questions.audience: SBC pastor or member weighing how to respond to denominational leadership
questions.answer: Deevers privately texted Barber requesting a conversation when the original attack hit; Barber ignored him. After the partial 'repentance,' Deevers again asked publicly for a private conversation, and Barber again ignored him, indicating the apology was a performance rather than restoration directed at the wronged brother.
questions.quote: When Barber first launched his attack, Deevers texted him politely asking for a conversation about Barber's accusations. Barber ignored him. After Barber's partial, performative repentance, Deevers [again asked Barber for a private conversation](https://twitter.com/DustyDeevers/status/1583526540041936896). Once again, the victim of slander was ignored by the slanderer.

questions.question: Why does the article treat Barber's continued accusations against Deevers as proof of unrepentance?
questions.audience: Pastor or counselor teaching biblical repentance
questions.answer: Inside the apology itself Barber twice charged Deevers with 'hyperbolic incivility' and once with 'unwarranted and unprovoked attacks' on Leatherwood, the same finger-pointing parents teach children to drop when apologizing. Silberman argues this displays disdain for the victim and zero sorrow for the damage caused.
questions.quote: The 'repentance' serves not Deevers, but Barber, by continuing to malign Deevers while Barber feigns humility before his Twitter followers. His thread is the opposite of repentance. It is nothing but a continuation of his brutal character assault on Deevers thinly veiled in a display of worldly grief and performative repentance.

questions.question: How central is repentance to the abolitionist movement, according to the article?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian unfamiliar with the Louisiana bill controversy
questions.answer: Silberman calls repentance a defining theme and effectively a rite of passage into abolitionism, noting that the 2022 Abolition Now Conference's theme was 'Repent With Us' and featured public repentance testimonies from leaders like T. Russell Hunter, Blake Gideon, and Jon Speed.
questions.quote: Repentance is so central to abolitionism that the theme of the 2022 Abolition Now Conference was 'Repent With Us', and featured public [repentance testimonies](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Lot-1dC1fyE) from abolitionist leaders like T. Russell Hunter, Blake Gideon, and Jon Speed.

questions.question: What does the addendum say about how abolition bills actually treat coerced abortive mothers?
questions.audience: Skeptic of abolitionism investigating claims about coerced minors and ectopic pregnancies
questions.answer: Most abolition bills restate or strengthen existing duress law so that anyone coerced into committing a crime is afforded the same protection as in any other criminal case. Whatever immunity or affirmative-defense framework a state already provides for coerced defendants applies equally to coerced abortive mothers, contrary to Barber's framing of unequal treatment.
questions.quote: Most abolition bills include restatements of existing law which protect those who commit acts under duress, and some abolition bills amend existing duress language to offer clearer, surer protection to the coerced. The exact protection that anyone coerced into committing a crime would receive would be afforded to women coerced into abortion.

questions.question: Why does Silberman tie the discussion of repentance to revival in the SBC?
questions.audience: SBC pastor or member weighing how to respond to denominational leadership
questions.answer: He argues every revival in scripture begins with repentance, and the SBC, riddled with worldliness, compromise, malice, and hatred, will not be reformed without it. Barber, as president, has the chance to lead the convention by example.
questions.quote: It's no secret to anyone that the SBC is not a healthy denomination. Worldliness, compromise, malice, and hatred are running rampant through the convention. If a sovereign act of God is coming to bring reformation and revival, it will begin with repentance. Barber has an opportunity to lead by example.

related.title: SBC President Slanders SBC Pastor
related.reason: The original Free the States report on Barber's September 2022 attack that this article is responding to.

related.title: Barber/ERLC Swing and Miss (Again) at Abolitionism
related.reason: Continues the Barber/ERLC narrative into 2023 and shows the pattern of pro-life establishment opposition this article documents.

related.title: Bible or Barber on Abolishing Abortion?
related.reason: Dusty Deevers's own follow-up confronting Barber's theology of incrementalism, written by the pastor Barber slandered.

related.title: On Repentance, Abolitionists Must Lead by Example: An Open Letter
related.reason: Lays out the abolitionist theology of repentance the article appeals to as the standard Barber failed to meet.

related.title: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
related.reason: Develops the link between incrementalist legislation (Barber's defended position) and the corrosion of genuine repentance.

related.title: Abolition of Abortion in Louisiana Act Passes Committee!
related.reason: Provides context for the Louisiana bill whose defeat is at the heart of the Leatherwood/ERLC controversy Barber tried to sidestep.

related.title: Fruits of Abolitionism: Is True Repentance Necessary?
related.reason: Companion theological treatment of what genuine repentance requires within the abolitionist movement.
